songs.title: B-B-Big!
songs.lyrics:
[Intro]
B! B! B-b-b!
(b… b… b…)

[Hook]
B! B! B-b-b!
What begins with B?
B-b-ball! B-b-ball!
It's a ball! (a ball!)

[Verse 2]
B! B! B-b-b!
What begins with B?
B-b-bear! B-b-bear!
It's a bear! (a bear!)

[Verse 3]
B! B! B-b-b!
What begins with B?
B-b-boat! B-b-boat!
It's a boat! (a boat!)

[Verse 4]
B! B! B-b-b!
What begins with B?
B-b-bird! B-b-bird!
It's a bird! (a bird!)

[Bridge]
A potato?! (NO!)
P-p-potato! Not B!
(b… b… b…)

[Outro]
B-b-big! The dog is BIG!
B-b-big! (big, big, big!)
B! B! B-b-b!
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: The B sound is a voiced bilabial stop /b/ — lips pop apart with the voice on. NEVER 'bee' (letter name) and NEVER 'buh' (schwa). Watch-week note: b is being learned three weeks after d, so the '(b… b… b…)' drone is deliberately voiced and rounded to CONTRAST the flatter d. All basket words are hard clean /b/. Answer line reuses the Week 1 frame.

songs.title: The Big Dog
songs.lyrics:
[Intro]
(big… big… big…)

[Hook]
The big dog! The big dog!
How big is the dog?
As big as a pig! (a pig!)
The dog is big, big, big!

[Verse 2]
The big dog! The big dog!
How big is the dog?
As big as a cat! (a cat!)
The dog is big, big, big!

[Verse 3]
The big dog! The big dog!
How big is the dog?
As big as a bus! (a bus!)
The dog is big, big, big!

[Verse 4]
A cat! A little cat!
The cat is mad! (hiss!)
The big dog hid! (he hid?!)
The big dog hid, hid, hid!

[Outro]
The big dog and the potato…
(POTATO!)
The big dog hid! (shhh…)
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: ONE frame: 'The dog is big, big, big!' with 'as big as a ___' swapping. The gag lands in verse 4 and the outro — the enormous dog is scared of the tiny cat (and the potato). Whisper the outro. Great b/d listening drill: 'big' and 'dog' sit side by side all song.

spreads.artNote: The Dog, but MASSIVE — filling the frame, gentle eyes, comically oversized. Set the joke: he is very, very big.
The big dog towering over a tiny classroom, a child-sized chair between his paws for scale.
A normal, ordinary pig standing alone — small and calm.

spreads.text: The dog is big. The pig is not.
spreads.artNote: The huge dog beside the little pig for size comedy. 'The pig is not' = the pig is not big.
A small tabby cat, tail flicking, eyeing the dog. Cast callback.

spreads.text: The big dog and the cat…
spreads.artNote: The enormous dog leaning down nervously toward the tiny, bold cat. Tension building.
The little cat arched, hissing, fierce. Tiny but terrifying.
THE JOKE: the giant dog cowering behind a tiny plant / under a small mat, paws over his eyes, hiding from the little cat. Page-turn payoff.

spreads.text: The dog is big. It is not.
spreads.artNote: The potato sits calmly in the foreground; the huge dog peeks around the corner behind it, still hiding. No potato-word printed — 'It is not' (not big) points at the tiny potato while the class SHOUTS the name. One eye of the dog visible.

sentences: A big dog and a big pig.
extras: 🚨 b/d WATCH-WEEK sort (the whole point of the week): word cards laid side by side — bad/dad, big/dig, bag/dig, bat/dad — with the b and d printed in contrasting colours; child reads and sorts by the starting sound. b = 'bat and ball first, then the ball' (line down, ball on the right); d = 'drumstick first, then the drum' (line down, belly on the left)
Moveable alphabet: build b-i-g, b-a-g, b-a-t, b-a-d — then swap the b for d and hear it change (bad → dad, big → dig)
Sandpaper letters b AND d traced in the SAME session, back to back, saying the sound each time — the confusion is met head-on, not avoided
Bingo caller deliberately mixes bag/dog and big/pig to sharpen the ear

teacherFocus: THE B/D WATCH-WEEK — b arrives three weeks after d, so this week's whole job is discrimination, not avoidance. Teach the voiced /b/ (lips pop, never 'buh'), then put b and d nose to nose: trace both sandpaper letters back to back, sort bad/dad and big/dig, and let the book do quiet work by parking 'big' and 'dog' side by side on every page. Use the formation stories the class already knows ('bat-and-ball' for b, 'drum-and-drumstick' for d). The giant-dog-scared-of-the-tiny-cat joke keeps the mood light while the ears do serious work.
